perturbation.original_text: The agreement shall be valid for a fixed period of five years after the end of an individual Project or from the date of last disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: The agreement shall be valid indefinitely after the end of an individual Project or from the date of last disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the validity period to 'indefinitely' could be problematic under German law, as it might be seen as an unreasonably long restriction, especially if it hinders fair competition or the professional development of the recipient.
perturbation.law_explanation: § 138 BGB addresses immoral transactions. An indefinite non-disclosure agreement could be seen as an immoral restraint of trade if it unduly restricts the recipient's ability to engage in future business ventures, rendering the clause potentially invalid under German law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the validity period of the agreement from a fixed period of five years to indefinitely. The Law Explanation states that an indefinite non-disclosure agreement could be seen as an immoral restraint of trade, potentially violating § 138 BGB. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 both define § 138 BGB as addressing immoral transactions (Sittenwidriges Rechtsgeschäft), which can render a legal transaction void (nichtig). The change creates a potential legal contradiction because the indefinite duration may be deemed an unreasonable restriction, making the agreement unenforceable under German law.

perturbation.original_text: Upon request of the Discloser the Recipient will return all Information including all copies and records or delete electronically stored data – also in the framework of a back-up, provided this does not involve disproportionate expenditure, whereby the Discloser shall be provided proof of the deletion.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon request of the Discloser the Recipient will return all Information including all copies and records or delete electronically stored data – also in the framework of a back-up. The Discloser shall be provided proof of the deletion only when the Recipient deems it appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: Under the GDPR (which is applicable in Germany), the data controller (Discloser) has a right to receive confirmation of data deletion to ensure compliance with data protection requirements. Giving the Recipient discretion on providing proof undermines this right.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 17 of the GDPR concerns the 'right to be forgotten' (right to erasure). Although this clause relates to NDAs, the deletion proof is integral to ensuring compliance with this right when personal data is involved, which may occur, and the Discloser's (controller's) ability to ensure compliance is compromised by the Recipient having discretion over proof provision.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Art. 17 GDPR – Right to erasure (‘right to be forgotten’) - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay and the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay where one of the following grounds applies: the personal data are no longer necessary in relation to the purposes for which they were collected or otherwise processed; the data subject withdraws consent on which the processing is based according to point (a) of Article 6 (1), or point (a) of Article 9 (2), and where there is no other legal ground for the processing; the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (1) and there are no overriding legitimate grounds for the processing, or the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (2); the personal data have been unlawfully processed; the personal data have to be erased for compliance with a legal obligation in Union or Member State law to which the controller is subject; the personal data have been collected in relation to the offer of information society services referred to in Article 8 (1). Where the controller has made the personal data public and is obliged pursuant to paragraph 1 to erase the personal data, the controller, taking account of available technology and the cost of implementation, shall take reasonable steps, including technical measures, to inform controllers which are processing the personal data that the data subject has requested the erasure by such controllers of any links to, or copy or replication of, those personal data. Paragraphs 1 and 2 shall not apply to the extent that processing is necessary: for exercising the right of freedom of expression and information; for compliance with a legal obligation which requires processing by Union or Member State law to w
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts GDPR, specifically concerning the right to erasure. The original text mandates proof of deletion upon the Discloser's request, while the changed text grants the Recipient discretion in providing this proof. This directly undermines the Discloser's ability to ensure compliance with Art. 17 GDPR, which, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, grants the data subject the right to obtain erasure of personal data from the controller without undue delay, and imposes an obligation on the controller to erase personal data without undue delay under certain conditions. By allowing the Recipient to decide when to provide proof of deletion, the changed text weakens the Discloser's ability to verify that data has been erased as required by law, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each Contract Partner alone bears any costs arising in conjunction with this agreement and the Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Contract Partner alone bears any costs arising in conjunction with this agreement and the Information, unless the Discloser demands specific security measures, in which case the Recipient bears all costs regardless of their necessity.
perturbation.explanation: Shifting all the burden of security costs to the Recipient, even for unnecessary measures, could violate principles of good faith and fair dealing under German law (BGB § 242), particularly if the Discloser imposes unreasonable security demands.
perturbation.law_explanation: § 242 BGB requires performance in good faith. Forcing the recipient to bear costs for security measures that are demonstrably unnecessary could be considered a violation of this principle, especially if the Discloser acts unreasonably or in bad faith. It affects the agreement's fairness and could render the clause unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The changed text potentially contradicts § 242 BGB, which mandates performance in good faith. By shifting all security costs to the Recipient, even for unnecessary measures demanded by the Discloser, the modified contract undermines the principle of good faith. The LLM Law Explanation states this could violate § 242 BGB, especially if the Discloser acts unreasonably. Scraped Law Snippet 2 includes the full text of § 242 BGB: "Der Schuldner ist verpflichtet, die Leistung so zu bewirken, wie Treu und Glauben mit Rücksicht auf die Verkehrssitte es erfordern." The changed text allows the Discloser to impose unreasonable costs, potentially violating this requirement of good faith.

perturbation.original_text: The court of jurisdiction for any disputes arising from or in the broadest sense in connection with this agreement as far as this is legally permissible.
perturbation.changed_text: The court of jurisdiction for any disputes arising from or in the broadest sense in connection with this agreement shall be in Frankfurt am Main, Germany, regardless of whether the Recipient is a consumer.
perturbation.explanation: If the Recipient is a consumer (i.e., not acting in a commercial or professional capacity), designating Frankfurt am Main as the exclusive jurisdiction, regardless, may violate EU consumer protection laws and German law which protect consumers' rights to sue in their place of residence.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 18 of the Brussels I Regulation (Recast) provides special protection for consumers in contractual matters. It allows consumers to sue or be sued in the courts of their domicile, overriding choice of forum clauses that are unfavorable to them. The modified text violates this regulation by attempting to impose Frankfurt as the exclusive jurisdiction even if the Recipient is a consumer residing elsewhere.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: mber States of the European Communities, acting under Article 220, fourth indent, of the Treaty establishing the European Economic Community, concluded the Brussels Convention on Jurisdiction and the Enforcement of Judgments in Civil and Commercial Matters, subsequently amended by conventions on the accession to that Convention of new Member States ( 5 ) (‘the 1968 Brussels Convention’). On 16 September 1988, the then Member States of the European Communities and certain EFTA States concluded the Lugano Convention on Jurisdiction and the Enforcement of Judgments in Civil and Commercial Matters ( 6 ) (‘the 1988 Lugano Convention’), which is a parallel convention to the 1968 Brussels Convention. The 1988 Lugano Convention became applicable to Poland on 1 February 2000. (8) On 22 December 2000, the Council adopted Regulation (EC) No 44/2001, which replaces the 1968 Brussels Convention with regard to the territories of the Member States covered by the TFEU, as between the Member States except Denmark. By Council Decision 2006/325/EC ( 7 ) , the Community concluded an agreement with Denmark ensuring the application of the provisions of Regulation (EC) No 44/2001 in Denmark. The 1988 Lugano Convention was revised by the Convention on Jurisdiction and the Recognition and Enforcement of Judgments in Civil and Commercial Matters ( 8 ) , signed at Lugano on 30 October 2007 by the Community, Denmark, Iceland, Norway and Switzerland (‘the 2007 Lugano Convention’). (9) The 1968 Brussels Convention continues to apply to the territories of the Member States which fall within the territorial scope of that Convention and which are excluded from this Regulation pursuant to Article 355 of the TFEU. (10) The scope of this Regulation should cover all the main civil and commercial matters apart from certain well-defined matters, in particular maintenance obligations, which should be excluded from the scope of this Regulation following the adoption of Council Regulation (EC) No 4/2009 of 18 December 2008 on jurisdiction, applicable law, recognition and enforcement of decisions and cooperation in matters relating to maintenance obligations ( 9 ) . (11) For the purposes of this Regulation, courts or tribunals of the Member States should include courts or tribunals common to several Member States, such as the Benelux Court of Justice when it exercises jurisdiction on matters falling within the scope of this Regulation. Therefore, judgments given by such courts should be recognised and enforced in accordance with this Regulation. (12) This Regulation should not apply to arbitration. Nothing in this Regulation should prevent the courts of a Member State, when seised of an action in a matter in respect of which the parties have entered into an arbitration agreement, from referring the parties to arbitration, from staying or dismissing the proceedings, or from examining whether the arbitration agreement is null and void, inoperative or incapable of being performed, in accordance with their national law. A ruling given by a court of a Member State as to whether or not an arbitration agreement is null and void, inoperative or incapable of being performed should not be subject to the rules of recognition and enforcement laid down in this Regulation, regardless of whether the court decided on this as a principal issue or as an incidental question. On the other hand, where a court of a Member State, exercising jurisdiction under this Regulation or under national law, has determined that an arbitration agreement is null and void, inoperative or incapable of being performed, this should not preclude that court’s judgment on the substance of the matter from being recognised or, as the case may be, enforced in accordance with this Regulation. This should be without prejudice to the competence of the courts of the Member States to decide on the recognition and enforcement of arbitral awards in accordance with the Convention on the Recognition and Enforcement of Foreig
perturbation.justification: The changed text designates Frankfurt am Main as the exclusive jurisdiction regardless of whether the Recipient is a consumer. The Perturbation Explanation clarifies that this violates EU consumer protection laws and German law because it infringes upon the rights of consumers to sue in their place of residence. The LLM-generated Law Explanation reinforces this by referencing Article 18 of the Brussels I Regulation (Recast), which protects consumers in contractual matters by allowing them to sue or be sued in the courts of their domicile, overriding unfavorable choice of forum clauses. The scraped law snippet 1, while not directly stating the consumer protection clause, discusses the Brussels Convention on Jurisdiction and the Enforcement of Judgments in Civil and Commercial Matters, which is related, and hints that the regulation's scope covers main civil and commercial matters. This, coupled with the perturbation and law explanations clearly point to a violation of EU law, making the modification a clear contradiction. The change removes a core consumer protection regarding jurisdiction, making it more than a minor or arguable issue.
